questions.question: Explain the differences between IPv4 and IPv6 headers. Why is the IPv6 header considered simpler?
keyPoints: IPv4 header is 20-60 bytes with many fields; IPv6 header is fixed at 40 bytes
IPv6 removes checksum, fragmentation fields, and options from main header
IPv6 introduces Flow Label for QoS and uses Extension Headers for optional features
Simplified processing at routers leads to faster packet handling

questions.question: What is CIDR notation? How does it help in efficient IP address allocation?
keyPoints: CIDR uses IP/prefix length notation (e.g., /24) instead of classful boundaries
Allows subnetting beyond traditional class boundaries
Reduces routing table size through route aggregation (supernetting)
Enables efficient utilization of limited IPv4 address space

questions.question: Describe the three transition mechanisms from IPv4 to IPv6.
keyPoints: Dual Stack: Device runs both IPv4 and IPv6 stacks simultaneously
Tunneling: Encapsulates IPv6 traffic inside IPv4 packets for transport across IPv4 networks
Translation: Converts between IPv4 and IPv6 at the network layer (NAT64, etc.)

questions.question: Explain the concept of private IP addresses and their role in IPv4 networks.
keyPoints: Not routable on the public internet, used for internal networks
NAT allows private hosts to share public IP for internet access
Solves short-term address exhaustion but creates connectivity complexity

questions.question: What are the key improvements of IPv6 over IPv4?
keyPoints: 128-bit address space (virtually unlimited addresses)
Built-in IPsec support for security
Simplified header structure for faster processing
Auto-configuration (SLAAC) and renumbering capabilities
No broadcast, efficient multicast support
Built-in QoS with Flow Label field

questions.question: Explain the IPv6 address types: Unicast, Multicast, and Anycast.
keyPoints: Unicast: Identifies a single interface - packet delivered to exactly one node
Multicast: Identifies group of interfaces - packet delivered to all members
Anycast: Identifies group of interfaces - packet delivered to nearest one (in terms of routing)
IPv6 has no broadcast - multicast used instead for similar functions

questions.question: Given the IP address 192.168.50.130/26, determine: (i) Network address (ii) Broadcast address (iii) First usable host (iv) Last usable host (v) Number of usable hosts
keyPoints: /26 means 26 bits for network, 6 bits for hosts
Network: 192.168.50.128 (stay within 64-address block)
Broadcast: 192.168.50.191 (last address in block)
First usable: 192.168.50.129
Last usable: 192.168.50.190
Number of usable hosts: 2^6 - 2 = 62

questions.question: Compare and contrast IPv4 and IPv6 protocols with respect to addressing, routing, security, and transition challenges.
keyPoints: Addressing: IPv4 (32-bit, ~4B addresses) vs IPv6 (128-bit, ~340 trillion addresses)
Routing: IPv4 uses classful/CIDR, IPv6 uses hierarchical routing with aggregated prefixes
Security: IPv4 requires additional protocols (IPsec), IPv6 has built-in IPsec support
Header: IPv4 (20-60 bytes, complex) vs IPv6 (40 bytes, simplified with extension headers)
Auto-configuration: DHCP-dependent IPv4 vs SLAAC in IPv6
Transition: Complex due to huge installed base of IPv4 infrastructure

questions.question: Explain the Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP) in IPv6 and its functions.
keyPoints: Replaces ARP, ICMP Router Discovery, and Redirect messages of IPv4
Router Solicitation: Hosts request router information
Router Advertisement: Routers announce presence and parameters
Neighbor Solicitation: Similar to ARP request for address resolution
Neighbor Advertisement: Response to solicitation
DAD (Duplicate Address Detection): Ensures address uniqueness
NDP uses ICMPv6 messages with specific types

questions.question: Design a subnetting scheme for an organization requiring the following: 3 subnets with 50 hosts each, 2 subnets with 100 hosts each, and 1 subnet with 200 hosts. Starting from the private network 192.168.0.0/24, create an efficient addressing scheme.
keyPoints: Calculate required host bits: 200 hosts need 8 bits (2^8-2=254) so use /24 as base
Need 6 subnets total - use 3 bits for subnetting (/27 = 30 hosts too small, /26 = 62 sufficient for smaller subnets)
Break into: 2 subnets of /25 (128 hosts each) - assign to 200-host requirement
Remaining: split into /26 subnets (62 hosts each) for 100-host needs
Remaining: split into /27 subnets (30 hosts each) for 50-host needs
